When a project for Mercor ends, managers often post a link to other projects on the platform and encourage people to apply. “But again, there are thousands of people applying, so you throw your application into a hole and hope to hear back at some undefined point,” says Katya. While they wait, workers sign up for Handshake, Micro1, Alignerr, or another of the ever-growing number of data providers.These companies are always recruiting. Like Mercor, many use AI interviewers and automated evaluations, meaning they have no incentive to limit the number of interviews they do. Mercor offers referral bonuses of several hundred dollars, leading some to promote the company so aggressively that mentions of it have been banned from several subreddits. Katya has applied for dozens of jobs and gotten three, not an unusual ratio.Nor do companies bear any cost for overhiring. Because workers are ostensibly independent contractors, they are not owed paid time off, breaks, healthcare, overtime pay, or unemployment benefits. It’s free to keep them hanging around, and a surplus of vetted workers ensures they will jump quickly to finish tasks before someone else does. It all combines to create an arrangement in which employers can turn labor on and off like a tap. (Reached for comment, Mercor spokesperson Heidi Hagberg said that “the nature of this is project based contract work, meaning it can extend, pause, or end at any time, especially as the client’s scopes and needs evolve,” and that many of the worker complaints “were centered around the misalignment of expectations of a full-time job versus -project-based work.”)If you move fast and get lucky and have the right combination of expertise and stay on the right side of each platform’s unique and mysterious recipe of productivity metrics, you can make decent money. I spoke to a playwright making $10,000 a month, a multitalented chemist who at various points found gigs demonstrating poker and singing for AI. But even then, there is an inescapable awareness of ephemerality because producing training data means working toward your own obsolescence. While the number of people doing data work may continue to rise, any particular gig will last only as long as it takes for the machines to successfully mimic it. It takes years for a human to develop expertise, and sooner or later, they’re going to run out of skills to sell.A worker with a master’s in linguistics had found steady rubric work for a year, but late in 2025, he noticed it was becoming more difficult to stump the models. Any obscure theory or Indigenous language he asked about, the model would find the correct papers. Instead of submitting three or four rubrics per week, he was lucky to get one. Everyone else on the project was following the same trajectory, so he wasn’t surprised when it came to an end. Their know-how had been extracted. In the past, he’d always been able to find a new gig, but now when he looked around, he saw only requests for medical experts, human-resources managers, and teachers. He has now been without work for five months and isn’t sure what to do next.Early labor skirmishes are already happening, mostly in California, which has some of the most aggressive rules around classifying platform workers. Three class-action lawsuits have been filed against Mercor in the past six months. (Similar suits were previously filed against Surge AI and Scale AI, which is settling.) The lawsuits all accuse the companies of misclassifying workers as independent contractors given the “extraordinary control” they exert over them. This is “an entirely new kind of work,” one that the company trains people to do and that cannot be done except on the company’s platform. Workers have so little visibility into what they’re working on that one person, alleges a suit filed in December, accepted a Mercor project only to be tasked with recording himself reading sexually explicit scripts. Once he discovered this, the worker risked deactivation if he abandoned the project, forcing him to “choose between being paid and being humiliated.”These companies are reminiscent of Uber and Lyft a decade ago, says Glenn Danas, a partner at the law firm Clarkson, which is suing Mercor and several other data platforms. Yet in some ways these workers are in a worse position, more replaceable despite their advanced degrees. Uber drivers have to be physically present in a city to work, and they can organize and push for regulation there. If the same were to happen with data workers, companies could just recruit from somewhere else where people will work for less. When Mercor cut pay for its Meta project to $16 per hour, it dropped below the minimum wage in California and other states, yet people there kept working because they needed the money. This was something at least one supervisor acknowledged, writing in Slack, “While we won’t actively hire from any states where the minimum wage is above the project’s rate, if you are already active on the project and would like to work at the $16/hr rate, we want to enable you to do so.”Entire professions risk a similar race to the bottom, says Acemoglu, if companies are able to pit workers against one another, each selling their data before someone else can underbid them. “We may also need unionlike organizations that exercise some sort of collective ownership and prevent any kind of simple divide-and-rule strategies by large companies to drive down data prices,” he says. “If there isn’t the legal infrastructure for a data economy of this sort, many of the people who produce the data will be underpaid or, to use a more loaded term, exploited.”Katya was among the thousands of people invited to join the $16-an-hour Project Nova and was appalled by the low pay. “I think that was Mercor’s experiment in how close to the bottom they can scrape without jeopardizing the data that they’re getting,” she says. Her main project had been paused for weeks and might resume the next day or never.In the end, she decided the money wasn’t worth it. She applied to work at a local coffee shop. It wasn’t the career pivot she’d imagined when she went to grad school; she just hoped working as a barista would be more stable. “At least when you work at a coffee shop for minimum wage, you have some friends to talk to and a boss who pretends to care about you. You have some kind of security; you know what your hours are going to be week to week,” she says.But then she heard her phone ding. One of her projects was back on.
history.author: Josh Dzieza
history.descriptionZh:
本文深度揭示了人工智能训练数据行业背后的人类劳动链条，呈现了一个由技术颠覆催生的新兴就业市场及其暗藏的矛盾。失业的原文案撰稿人Katya，在财务压力下接受了一家名为Mercor的公司的AI面试，由此进入了一个为大型AI实验室（如OpenAI和Anthropic）生产训练数据的隐秘世界。

**核心技术原理与行业背景**
以ChatGPT为代表的大语言模型的惊人流畅度，并非完全自主生成，而是依赖于一个庞大的人类反馈强化学习（RLHF）流程。起初，AI公司雇佣人类撰写示例对话并对模型回答进行评级。然而，行业发现仅靠这种基础评分训练出的模型虽然听起来聪明，却不可靠。软件工程领域因有明确的“代码能否编译运行”的客观反馈而成为例外，模型可通过试错达到真正可用。

为了解决其他领域缺乏客观标准的问题，AI公司开始投入巨资，雇佣各行业的专业人士来创建极其详尽、严苛的“评价标准”（rubrics）和“黄金答案”（golden answers），旨在教会AI如何才算“做好”一项工作，无论是撰写财务报告、创作广告文案，还是分析商业决策。

**行业规模与工作模式**
Mercor、Scale AI和Surge AI等数据公司构建了庞大的专家网络，号称拥有数十万包括硕士、博士、律师、咨询顾问、艺术家在内的专业人才。工作流程通常是流水线式的：一组专家（如律师或矿业分析师）进行“世界构建”，即模拟真实商业场景，生成大量虚构的会议记录、幻灯片和财务预测文档。随后，另一组专家则利用这些材料，试图设计出能“难倒”当前AI模型的“棘手问题”，接着再为这些难题撰写更细致的解答标准。

这个行业吸引了大量高学历却就业不足或面临失业的专业人士。随着传统行业岗位减少，生产AI训练数据成了许多人的无奈选择，他们中不乏获奖的编剧、导演、资深设计师和教师。

**残酷的劳动生态：不稳定、高压与监控**
尽管工作内容涉及高级专业知识，但从业者却处于“零工经济”的最极端形态：

1.  **极度不稳定**：项目随AI公司的数据需求变化而随时启动、暂停或终止，毫无预警。工作者形容自己就像“鱼缸里的鱼”，等待随时可能投喂的食物，只有最快抢到的人才能“吃到”。
2.  **薪酬递减与要求递增**：几乎所有的受访工作者都表示，随着项目推进，薪酬下降、任务时间缩短、要求却提高。例如，一个标注Instagram视频的项目在无预警中断后重启，时薪从21美元降至16美元，五千名工人被转移至新的Slack群组，陷入混乱。
3.  **严密监控与高压管理**：工作者被要求安装如Insightful等监控软件，跟踪每一秒的活动。系统会因短暂无键盘操作而发出询问，被判定为“不具生产力”的时间会被扣薪。这导致工作者被迫在“下班时间”无偿阅读任务说明以加快速度，甚至因压力过大而向家人发火。
4.  **年轻且缺乏经验的管理层**：由三位20岁出头的创始人领导的Mercor，其管理层普遍年轻且缺乏大规模项目管理经验。工作者抱怨指导混乱，沟通常以表情包敷衍，资深专业人士需要向几乎不懂该领域的年轻经理汇报，并面临因“速度不够快”而被“强制离职”的威胁。
5.  **保密文化与权力不对等**：所有项目均使用代号，客户身份严格保密。工作者签署的保密协议禁止他们透露工作细节甚至客户名称，这使得他们无法凭借经验在市场上为自己定价，也无法组织起来争取权益。这种沉默成为AI公司维持低价和高度控制的关键。
6.  **自我颠覆的悖论**：工作者清醒地意识到，他们正在训练替代自己（或同行）的AI。一位语言学硕士发现，随着模型能力提升，他越来越难提出能难倒AI的问题，最终项目因“专业知识已被提取完毕”而结束，他随之陷入长期失业。

**深层矛盾与未来隐忧**
报道揭示了AI发展的一个核心悖论：一方面，AI实验室描绘着替代人类认知劳动的通用智能远景；另一方面，它们正斥巨资一项一项地自动化具体任务，其方式是雇佣大量人类来教导AI如何完成这些任务。

这带来的并非突然的大规模失业，而是一种更微妙但同样具有破坏性的未来：越来越多人从事的工作，就是教会AI做自己曾经做的工作。这种模式正在将知识工作转变为类似网约车司机和外卖骑手的高度不稳定、被算法监控的平台零工。

**法律纠纷与结构性问题**
近期，加州已出现针对Mercor等公司的集体诉讼，指控其将受严格控制的工人错误归类为“独立承包商”，以规避最低工资、加班费和福利等法律义务。然而，数据工作者比网约车司机更易被替代，因为他们不受地理位置限制，公司可以全球招募要价更低的人选。

经济学家担心，如果缺乏法律框架和集体议价机制，整个专业领域可能陷入“逐底竞争”，从业者被迫竞相低价出售自己的专业知识数据，最终导致系统性剥削。

**结论**
Katya的故事是一个缩影。在经历了收入尚可但压力巨大的数据标注工作后，面对时薪被大幅削减的新项目，她最终选择申请当地咖啡店的工作，寻求一种尽管薪酬低但更具人性和稳定性的生活。然而，当她原来的项目突然恢复时，那熟悉的提示音再次响起，凸显了这种不稳定经济形态对人持续不断的拉扯与诱惑。AI训练数据行业在加速技术进步的同时，也正在重塑劳动的形态与尊严，引发了关于技术红利分配、劳动保障和人类价值在智能时代定位的深刻拷问。

history.title: Nexperia China claims to be making chips using 12-inch wafers — subsidiary deepens split with Dutch parent as it builds out 'mass production capabilities'
history.description: In a statement posted to its Chinese social media account, Nexperia China said it had reached a new milestone in its "independent R&D and mass production capabilities".
history.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 2 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Tom's Hardware Get the Tom's Hardware Newsletter Get Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox. You are now subscribed Your newsletter sign-up was successful An account already exists for this email address, please log in. Subscribe to our newsletter Nexperia's Chinese subsidiary has announced that it has achieved small-batch production of chips using 12-inch silicon wafers, a development that deepens the split between the unit and its Dutch parent company, which does not manufacture wafers at that diameter.Go deeper with TH Premium: Chipmaking (Image credit: tsmc)A deeper look at the chipmaking supply chainTSMC's $165 billion U.S. investments examinedChina reportedly reverse-engineers EUV toolChina bets on DUV, as EUV blockade reshapes chipmakingIn a statement posted to its Chinese social media account, Nexperia China said it had reached a new milestone in its "independent R&D and mass production capabilities," per Reuters, producing bipolar discrete devices, Schottky rectifiers, and electrostatic discharge protection devices. All of these are commodity power semiconductors that the Dutch company also manufactures. Nexperia's European headquarters declined to comment, and Nexperia China could not immediately be reached to confirm where it is sourcing its 12-inch wafers.The most likely supplier is WingSkySemi, a 12-inch wafer fab in Shanghai operated by Wingtech founder Zhang Xuezheng. Back in December, Reuters reported on a letter sent to Nexperia China's distributors, which named WingSkySemi as the planned source of automotive-grade wafers, with a stated monthly capacity of 30,000 wafers, alongside 8-inch IGBT wafer supply from Shanghai GAT Semiconductor and United Nova Technology Co., a fab linked to SMIC.Article continues below You may like Nexperia China seeks new wafer suppliers amid legal standoff with Dutch parent, could take 6 months for qualification China warns the Netherlands to ‘immediately correct its mistakes’ over Nexperia saga that has disrupted auto production China warns of fresh chip shortage as Nexperia dispute escalates again - Dutch headquarters allegedly locked Chinese staff out of IT systems The chips that Nexperia China claims it can now produce are simpler than IGBTs, the insulated-gate bipolar transistors used for current regulation in applications like EVs and industrial equipment. That said, if true, the announcement demonstrates how far the company’s Chinese subsidiary has moved toward building a self-contained supply chain since its spat with Nexperia proper began in late 2025.That started when the Dutch government took control of Nexperia from Wingtech in October 2025, citing governance concerns, and subsequently installed a European management team. In October, the Dutch side halted wafer shipments to Nexperia's Chinese factories, citing nonpayment, while a Dutch court ordered Wingtech's founder removed as Nexperia CEO.Beijing then responded by restricting exports of Nexperia's finished chips, causing shortages that forced Honda to temporarily suspend production at factories in China and Japan. Both governments partially relaxed their measures in November, but legal proceedings and the internal battle over control have continued to rage on, with Nexperia saying in December that its Chinese subsidiary has shown "no intention to negotiate a short-term solution to restore the flow of chips to customers."Nexperia China has said it delivered more than 11 billion chips to over 800 customers since mid-October despite the disruption. A Dutch court hearing on control of the company was scheduled for January 14, but it’s not yet clear what followed that hearing, and no resolution to the corporate governance dispute has been publicly announced.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.

history.titleZh: 安世半导体中国宣称制造出12英寸晶圆芯片，子公司与荷兰母公司分歧加深并建设量产能力
history.descriptionZh:
**背景与冲突根源**

近日，Nexperia（安世半导体）的中国子公司通过其社交媒体账号宣布，已实现基于12英寸（300毫米）硅片的小批量芯片生产。这一进展标志着该中国子公司与其荷兰母公司之间的分裂进一步加深。自2025年底以来，双方因控制权问题陷入激烈纠纷。2025年10月，荷兰政府以治理问题为由，从闻泰科技（Wingtech）手中接管了Nexperia，并任命了欧洲管理团队。随后，荷兰方面以未付款为由停止向Nexperia在华工厂供应晶圆，荷兰法院亦下令免去闻泰创始人张学政的Nexperia CEO职务。作为回应，中国政府限制了Nexperia成品芯片的出口，一度导致本田在中国和日本的工厂因芯片短缺而暂停生产。尽管双方在11月部分放松了措施，但法律诉讼和控制权之争持续不断。

**技术进展与核心产品**

Nexperia中国宣称，其在“独立研发与量产能力”上达到了新里程碑。已实现小批量生产的具体产品包括：
*   **双极分立器件**
*   **肖特基整流器**
*   **静电放电保护器件**

这些都是**成熟的功率半导体器件**，其荷兰母公司同样生产这些产品。需要指出的是，这些芯片在技术复杂度上**低于绝缘栅双极晶体管**——后者是用于电动汽车、工业设备等进行电流调控的关键高端器件。

**供应链与产能分析**

荷兰总部对此不予置评，且中国子公司未立即确认其12英寸晶圆的来源。但根据路透社此前报道，最有可能的供应商是位于上海的**12英寸晶圆代工厂WingSkySemi**（由闻泰创始人张学政运作）。报道曾引述一份发给分销商的信件，其中指明WingSkySemi计划供应车规级晶圆，**月产能规划为3万片**。此外，供应链中还包括上海GAT半导体和与中芯国际有关联的United Nova Technology Co.，它们提供**8英寸IGBT晶圆**。

此次宣布的12英寸晶圆生产，其**意义不在于技术上的尖端突破**，而在于**供应链的“去荷兰化”**。这表明在遭遇母公司断供后，Nexperia中国正加速构建一个自主可控的本地化制造链条。

**性能与影响评估**

1.  **产能意义**：虽然是小批量，但结合WingSkySemi规划的3万片/月产能，显示了其向规模化生产迈进的潜力，旨在保障基础功率半导体器件的稳定供应。
2.  **市场影响**：
    *   **短期**：Nexperia中国声称，自2025年10月中旬以来，尽管存在纠纷，其仍向超过800家客户交付了**110亿颗芯片**。这有助于缓解部分因出口限制引发的市场短缺。
    *   **长期/战略影响**：此举可能使全球功率半导体供应链格局变得更加复杂和区域化。如果Nexperia中国成功建立独立于荷兰的完整产销体系，将成为一个具有相当影响力的区域性竞争者，特别是在中国市场。这也反映了在地缘政治紧张背景下，供应链安全优先于全球化效率的趋势。
3.  **应用场景**：所生产的器件广泛应用于**汽车电子、工业控制、消费电子**等领域，是基础但不可或缺的元件。实现本土化生产，有助于提升中国下游产业，尤其是汽车产业链在关键通用芯片上的韧性。

**当前状态与未来展望**

截至新闻发布时，围绕公司控制权的法律斗争仍未平息。原定于1月14日举行的一场荷兰法院听证会结果未明，公司治理纠纷尚未有公开的解决方案。Nexperia荷兰方面在去年12月曾表示，其中国子公司“无意谈判短期解决方案以恢复对客户的芯片供应”。

**总结而言**，Nexperia中国实现12英寸晶圆芯片量产，是其在与荷兰母公司决裂后寻求供应链自主的关键一步。这虽未涉及最前沿的芯片技术，但通过利用本土代工资源（如WingSkySemi），它正试图稳固其作为主要功率半导体供应商的地位。这一发展既是商业控制权争夺的产物，也嵌入了全球半导体产业重组与区域化的大背景之中，其最终走向将深刻影响全球功率半导体的竞争格局与供应链分布。
